SnowDistribution: A mainly dry but very windy day in Lochaber
Comment: Winds easing and a dry day in prospect
WeatherInfluences: After some light precipitation late on Tuesday, it will be mainly dry. The freezing level will lower to around 1100m during the day before rising slightly later. Winds will gradually ease.
SnowStability: The snowpack will stabilise in the dry and slightly cooler conditions. Snowpack stability will be generally good.  The avalanche hazard will be Low
ObservedWeatherInfluences: After a period of rain at all levels overnight, today has been generally dry with the freezing level well above the summits. Strong South-Westerly winds
ObservedSnowStability: After a period of high instability overnight, snowpack stability has gradually improved during the dryer conditions today. Remaining cornices are unstable and prone to collapse.  The avalanche hazard is Moderate

SnowDistribution: Wet and diminishing snowpack. Cloud level around the highest tops. Poor visibility in showers.  Winds approx 40-50 mph making progress very difficult and strenuous.
Comment: Sunshine and light winds forecast for Thursday.
WeatherInfluences: Light precipitation during the evening will fall as sleet on the highest summits. Becoming dry through the early hours and remaining so for the rest of the forecast period. Strong to gale force South-Westerly winds will ease overnight becoming moderate to light through the day.
SnowStability: Stability will be generally good as the snowpack continues to slowly thaw and consolidate. Above 600 metres the snow cover will be patchy with the greatest accumulations on North through East to South-East aspects above 800 metres. Any residual cornices will be prone to collapse.   The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Rain overnight at all levels then a mostly dry morning followed by further rain and drizzle during the afternoon. Storm force South-Westerly winds. The freezing level was above the summits with a temperature of 5 degrees recorded on Fionn Bheinn for much of the day.
ObservedSnowStability: The thawing snowpack is generally stable at all altitudes. Above 500 metres the snow cover is patchy. Greatest accumulations are on North through East to South East aspects above 900 metres. Residual old cornices are prone to collapse. The avalanche hazard is Low.

SnowDistribution: Wet snow throughout.  Cloud base was at 800 metres. Winds approx 40 - 50 mph.
Comment: Rock and ice fall will be a hazard in the mild conditions.
WeatherInfluences: After some overnight rain a generally dry day is expected. The freezing level will be just above the summits.
SnowStability: The snowpack will continue thawing and slowly consolidating. Some wet snow instabilities will remain on mainly on steep North to East to South-East aspects above 900 metres. Cornices above these aspects will remain unstable. On other aspects the snowpack will be generally stable. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: After some overnight rain it was a relatively dry day. It was also mild with the freezing level well above the tops. Winds were from the South-West.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack is thawing in the milder conditions. After a period of overnight instability the snowpack was slowly consolidating during the day. Instabilities remain on steep North through East to South-East aspects above 900 metres. Cornices above these aspects are prone to collapse. A number of small to medium size avalanches, probably corniced triggered last night, were observed on the aspects indicated previously. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.

SnowDistribution: Poor visibility in clouds and rain showers. Very strong Southerly winds 30-50 mph. Wet snow at all elevations, main snow cover now mostly above 800 metres with isolated patches at lower levels.
Comment: The wet snowpack slowly consolidating and stabilising.
WeatherInfluences: Rain will continue to fall at all elevations during Tuesday night with drier conditions expected during the day but remaining cloudy. The strong Southerly winds will ease and veer to be light, West-South-Westerly. The freezing level will generally remain above the summits but will briefly dip to 1200 metres in the early hours of the morning before rising again to 1600 metres later in the day.
SnowStability: Initially, wet snow instabilities on all steep slopes but more likely in sheltered gullies and steep slopes with a North-West through North to Easterly aspect and mainly above 850 metres.  Stability will start to improve as the day progresses as drier, cooler conditions take effect. Any remaining cornices will be weak and unstable. The avalanche hazard will be Moderate.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Last night and through today the freezing level has been well above the summits the midday summit temperature was plus 2.2 degrees Celsius with plus 13 degrees Celsius at sea level, last night even warmer temperatures affected the area. Rain at all elevations today accompanied by very strong Southerly winds have added to the rapid thaw in the area.
ObservedSnowStability: Wet snow instabilities were present on all steep slopes especially where deep accumulations of windslab previously existed. Sheltered gullies and steep slopes with a North-West through North to South-Easterly aspects above 700 metres where mostly affected, Cornices are still prone to collapse.  The avalanche hazard is Considerable.

SnowDistribution: Winds 75 mph and above, with a maximum gust of 102 mph recorded on Cairn Gorm.
Comment: A more settled regime will bring decreasing wind speeds and stable air resulting in a firm snowpack above 1000 metres by dawn.
WeatherInfluences: Rain overnight will dissipate leaving a dry day. Winds will be mostly from the South-West decreasing in strength from storm force to moderate by the afternoon. The freezing level will be around 1500 metres.
SnowStability: Lingering wet snow instabilities will dissipate as the day progresses. These will mostly be found on very steep slopes of a North through North-East to South-East aspect above 950 metres. During the day the snowpack will be subject to slow consolidation as temperatures fluctuate, with a firm snow surface above 1000 metres at dawn. Snow patches and ribbons will remain in depressions and hollows below 950 metres. Residual cornices will remain prone to collapse. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: Rain at all elevations overnight preceded light rain showers during the day. Winds were from the South-South-West and storm force for the duration of the period. A gust of 102 mph was recorded on Cairn Gorm at 1245 metres. The freezing level was well above the highest summits.
ObservedSnowStability: The snowpack has become moist on all aspects and elevations due to rain. A period of instability overnight has now passed leaving a slowly consolidating trend. The greatest extents of snow are found on North-West through North to East aspects above 950 metres, with ribbons and snow patches below. Residual cornices remain. The avalanche hazard is Moderate.

SnowDistribution: Most recent snow deposits are wet throughout. Surface layers on the older snowpack slowly softening. Cloud base was at 850 metres all day. Winds approx 40 - 50 mph hindering high level observations.
Comment: Rock and icefall a hazard in the mild conditions.
WeatherInfluences: Rain during the evening will clear to leave a mainly dry day with some light rain edging back in towards the end of the forecast period. The freezing level will be between 1400 and 1600 metres. Winds will be from the South-South-West with some fluctuation, easing through the night becoming light.
SnowStability: The most recent snow deposits will continue to consolidate and will be soft in nature. The old firm snowpack will thaw at a slower rate, especially above 1000 metres. In both cases, stability will be generally good. Greatest snow amounts will be found on high plateaus and in high North-West through North to East facing coires. Expect snow loss at lower levels. The avalanche hazard will be Low.
ObservedWeatherInfluences: A mild day with the freezing level remaining above the summits. Dry in the morning turning wet in the afternoon. Gale force South-Westerlies.
ObservedSnowStability: The most recent snow deposits continue to consolidate and are now soft in nature. The old firm snowpack is slowly thawing with only the surface layers becoming soft. In both cases, stability is generally good. At lower elevations, thaw conditions prevail with a loss of general snow cover. The avalanche hazard is Low.
